setting.note: Carpentier's northern Haiti runs from the sugar plantations of the Plaine du Nord and the colonial port of Cap-Français, through the night of the slave uprising, to the mountaintop kingdom that the freed slave Henri Christophe built for himself. The map holds the plain, the city now called Cap-Haïtien, the palace of Sans-Souci at Milot and the fortress of the Citadelle above it, with a single excursion across the water to the Cuban exile of Santiago.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Alejo Carpentier, first published in Spanish as El reino de este mundo, 1949. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Kingdom of This World, Alejo Carpentier (1949). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The oxblood red of revolt and of the brick the slaves carried up the mountain to raise the Citadelle. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
